tata.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2023 is consolidated while FY2024 is standalone

sections.about: This metric measures total waste generated across plastic, e-waste, biomedical, construction and demolition, battery, radioactive, hazardous and non-hazardous waste categories under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator on waste management. Total waste is reported with category-wise break-up under waste-management rules. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: In FY2025, Jindal Stainless reported the lowest total waste generated at 1,945,438 MT, followed by SAIL at 14,144,422 MT, Tata Steel at 17,352,473 MT and JSW at 19,109,339 MT. Tata Steel’s FY2025 increase from 16,833,524 MT in FY2024 was driven mainly by higher other non-hazardous waste, while its FY2023 value of 22,092,230 MT is not directly comparable because it is consolidated. Jindal’s structurally lower absolute waste reflects smaller scale and stainless/EAF scrap-based operations; JSL states that it uses scrap-based electric arc furnaces and promotes closed-loop recycling.
sections.tataPositioning: Tata ranked 3rd in FY2025 and generated about 792% more waste than the best performer, Jindal Stainless. Tata’s rank improved from 4th in FY2023 and FY2024 to 3rd in FY2025 because JSW’s waste generation rose sharply to 19.1 million MT, although Tata’s own absolute waste generation also worsened year-on-year.
sections.targets: SAIL states a corporate policy commitment to reduce solid waste generation and maximise utilisation to 100%, while JSL is working toward Zero-Waste-to-Landfill certification by 2030.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel FY2023 is consolidated, while FY2024 and FY2025 are standalone; all peer years are standalone.
